rationale: I split Economics & Finance into 14 practitioner-facing subspecialties to balance breadth and precision: enough cards to cover distinct research communities (theory, applied, policy, markets) without fragmenting rare niches. I kept econometrics as a stand-alone area because it underpins many papers and draws a large, cross-cutting audience; likewise Financial Economics is split from Corporate Finance & Accounting and Market Microstructure to respect distinct researcher/practitioner identities. I deliberately did not create separate tiny cards for narrow applied fields (e.g., sports economics, urban economics) — those fit under larger topical homes like Public, Labor, or Development — but I did split Monetary/Central Banking from Macroeconomics since central banking research has a distinct readership and policy focus.

subspecialties.name: Econometrics
subspecialties.tagline: Methods, identification, causal inference, and applied estimation
covers: Theoretical and applied estimation methods, identification strategies, causal inference
Examples: IV, RDD, panel methods, machine-learning for treatment effects
explicitly_excludes: Macro model estimation — goes to macroeconomics
Finance-specific asset pricing econometrics — goes to financial-economics

subspecialties.name: Financial Economics
subspecialties.tagline: Asset pricing, corporate valuation, risk and market behavior
covers: Asset pricing theory and empirical tests, risk premia, derivatives pricing
Examples: cross-section of returns, factor models, systemic risk
explicitly_excludes: Trading venue design and high-frequency execution — goes to market-microstructure
Corporate accounting topics — goes to corporate-finance-and-accounting

subspecialties.name: Macroeconomics
subspecialties.tagline: Growth, business cycles, fiscal policy, and macro theory
covers: Aggregate dynamics, DSGE models, fiscal policy, long-run growth
Examples: business-cycle transmission, savings and growth theory
explicitly_excludes: Monetary policy implementation and central bank operations — goes to monetary-and-central-banking

subspecialties.name: Microeconomics
subspecialties.tagline: Theory of individual and firm behavior, game theory, mechanism design
covers: Economic theory at individual/firm level, market design, game-theoretic models
Examples: auction theory, matching markets, contract theory
explicitly_excludes: Empirical IO papers — go to industrial-organization
Behavioral deviations — go to behavioral-economics

subspecialties.name: Behavioral Economics
subspecialties.tagline: Psychology-informed decision making, nudges, preferences and biases
covers: Empirical and experimental work on decision-making, heuristics, and interventions
Examples: lab/field experiments on biases, nudging policy studies
explicitly_excludes: Clinical or non-economics psychology studies — goes to Social Sciences
Behavioral asset pricing models tightly focused on markets — goes to financial-economics

subspecialties.name: Industrial Organization
subspecialties.tagline: Firm strategy, competition, antitrust, and market structure
covers: Firm behavior, entry, price setting, vertical contracts, regulation
Examples: antitrust empirical studies, oligopoly theory, platform competition
explicitly_excludes: Corporate accounting/finance topics — goes to corporate-finance-and-accounting
Macro-level industry growth — goes to macroeconomics or development

subspecialties.name: Labor Economics
subspecialties.tagline: Workforce, wages, unemployment, and labor-market policies
covers: Empirical and theoretical work on labor supply/demand, matching, training
Examples: minimum wage impacts, migration and employment, education returns
explicitly_excludes: Health-specific workforce studies — goes to health-economics
Macro unemployment dynamics — goes to macroeconomics

subspecialties.name: Public Economics
subspecialties.tagline: Taxation, government spending, social insurance, and public policy
covers: Design and effects of taxes, transfers, public goods, and regulation
Examples: optimal taxation, redistribution, evaluation of welfare programs
explicitly_excludes: Policy work without an economic framework — goes to Social Sciences
Public-health interventions with medical focus — goes to health-economics

subspecialties.name: Development Economics
subspecialties.tagline: Poverty, growth, institutions, and policy in low- and middle-income countries
covers: Micro and macro approaches to development, field experiments, institutions
Examples: cash transfer RCTs, credit constraints in developing economies
explicitly_excludes: Global macro/sovereign finance issues — goes to macroeconomics or financial-economics
Non-economic development studies — goes to Social Sciences

subspecialties.name: Corporate Finance & Accounting
subspecialties.tagline: Firm capital structure, governance, earnings, and financial reporting
covers: Corporate investment, payout policy, governance, accounting disclosures
Examples: earnings quality, mergers and acquisitions, CFO decision studies
explicitly_excludes: Asset-pricing or market-wide finance research — goes to financial-economics
Firm-level competition models — goes to industrial-organization

subspecialties.name: Market Microstructure & Trading
subspecialties.tagline: Trading, liquidity, exchanges, and high-frequency market dynamics
covers: How trading mechanisms shape prices, liquidity provision, execution costs
Examples: limit order book analysis, HFT effects, market design
explicitly_excludes: Long-horizon asset pricing — goes to financial-economics
Algorithmic learning methods without market context — goes to econometrics

subspecialties.name: Monetary Economics & Central Banking
subspecialties.tagline: Monetary policy, central bank operations, inflation and liquidity
covers: Policy rules, inflation dynamics, unconventional monetary tools, reserves
Examples: interest-rate pass-through, QE effectiveness, central-bank reports
explicitly_excludes: Broad macro growth theory — goes to macroeconomics
Banking regulation microprudential topics — goes to financial-economics

subspecialties.name: Health Economics
subspecialties.tagline: Healthcare markets, insurance, outcomes, and cost-effectiveness
covers: Economics of health care provision, insurance markets, and policy evaluation
Examples: provider incentives, drug pricing, health insurance reforms
explicitly_excludes: Clinical medical research — goes to Health & Medicine
Public-health-only policy studies without economic analysis — goes to Social Sciences

subspecialties.name: Environmental & Resource Economics
subspecialties.tagline: Climate, natural resources, environmental policy, and valuation
covers: Valuation of externalities, carbon pricing, renewable resources, conservation
Examples: carbon markets, cost-benefit of regulation, ecosystem service valuation
explicitly_excludes: Pure climate science — goes to Earth & Environmental Sciences
Energy engineering or technology design — goes to Engineering

uncovered_concerns: Urban, sports, and education economics are not separate cards; they were folded into Public, Labor, or Development to avoid many tiny specialty cards.
Behavioral finance was considered as its own card but is covered by either Financial Economics (market-focused) or Behavioral Economics (decision-focused) to keep the grid MECE.
Banking/regulation research spans Financial Economics and Monetary/Central Banking; chosen splits reflect typical practitioner audiences (market vs. policy/regulatory focus).
